content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

July 9, 1980

Mr. Robert J. Latham, Treasurer
THE COMMITTEE FOR THE PRESERVATION
OF CHAFFEY COLLEGE
7575 Alta Cuesta
Cucamonga, California 91730

Dear Mr. Latham:

Pursuant to your request, this letter is to confirm my advice to you during our telephone conversation of July 7, 1980.

During our conversation, you stated that you are the treasurer for "The Committee For The Preservation Of Chaffey College," (hereinafter referred to as "the committee") which has a surplus of approximately, twelve hundred dollars ($1,200.00). The committee has ceased all activity and wishes to dissolve. The funds were raised originally, for the sole purpose of paying for ads which protested actions taken by the local Board of Trustees.

You specifically asked, does the committee incur any filing obligations should they decide to contribute the balance of their surplus funds to a local recall committee which is in the process of organizing?

Since the committee did not raise the funds for "political purposes," which by adopted regulation is defined as being for the "purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure" (2 Cal.Admin. Code Section 18215), they were not obligated to file as a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act.

I advised you that the committee may contribute the balance of its surplus funds to the local recall committee without incurring a filing obligation under the Political Reform Act, unless the expenditures were five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) or more. Government Code Section 82013(c) defines a major donor as a committee which makes contributions directly to or at the behest of candidates and committees which total five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) or more in a calendar year. Since the

SACRAMENTO (916) 322 5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620 5196

Mr. Robert J. Latham
July 9, 1980
Page 2

twelve hundred dollars ($1,200.00) balance of surplus funds
falls far below the five thousand dollar ($5,000.00) reporting
threshold of a major donor committee, the Committee For the
Preservation of Chaffey College will not meet the filing
requirements and, therefore, not incur any filing obligations
under the Political Reform Act as a major donor committee.

In addition, since the contribution made to the local
recall committee will be over one hundred dollars ($100.00),
the recall committee which receives the contribution is
obligated under Government Code Section 84210(g) to identify
the Committee For the Preservation of Chaffey College as a
contributor to their committee.

I have enclosed a copy of the Political Reform Act for
your use and information. I hope that this information has
been helpful to you. Please feel free to contact me again
should you have any additional questions.

Sincerely,

Helen P. Arriola
Political Reform
Consultant

HPA:js

Enclosure

sections.question: Does the committee incur any filing obligations should they decide to contribute the balance of their surplus funds to a local recall committee which is in the process of organizing?
sections.conclusion: Since the committee did not raise the funds for 'political purposes' as defined by regulation, they were not obligated to file as a recipient committee. The committee may contribute the balance of its surplus funds to the local recall committee without incurring a filing obligation under the Political Reform Act, unless the expenditures were five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) or more. Since the twelve hundred dollars ($1,200.00) balance falls below the five thousand dollar ($5,000.00) reporting threshold of a major donor committee, the Committee For the Preservation of Chaffey College will not meet the filing requirements and will not incur any filing obligations under the Political Reform Act as a major donor committee. However, the recall committee receiving a contribution over one hundred dollars ($100.00) must identify the contributing committee.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a dissolved committee with a $1,200 surplus that originally raised funds to protest local Board of Trustees actions must file if it contributes its remaining funds to an organizing recall committee.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The committee has no filing obligations under the Political Reform Act because its original funds were not raised for political purposes and any contribution to the recall committee falls below the $5,000 major donor threshold. However, the receiving recall committee must identify the contributing committee in their filings.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains OCR artifacts (missing characters, formatting issues) but key sections are legible. Question section is identified from the letter body. Conclusion is synthesized from the advice paragraphs. Letter from Helen P. Arriola, Political Reform Consultant, dated July 9, 1980, referencing telephone conversation of July 7, 1980.

QUESTION: Does the committee incur any filing obligations should they decide to contribute the balance of their surplus funds to a local recall committee which is in the process of organizing?

CONCLUSION: Since the committee did not raise the funds for 'political purposes' as defined by regulation, they were not obligated to file as a recipient committee. The committee may contribute the balance of its surplus funds to the local recall committee without incurring a filing obligation under the Political Reform Act, unless the expenditures were five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) or more. Since the twelve hundred dollars ($1,200.00) balance falls below the five thousand dollar ($5,000.00) reporting threshold of a major donor committee, the Committee For the Preservation of Chaffey College will not meet the filing requirements and will not incur any filing obligations under the Political Reform Act as a major donor committee. However, the recall committee receiving a contribution over one hundred dollars ($100.00) must identify the contributing committee.

embedding.summary: FPPC advised a dissolving committee with a $1,200 surplus that it may contribute its funds to an organizing recall committee without incurring filing obligations, as the amount falls below the $5,000 major donor committee threshold and the original funds were not raised for political purposes.
